records.ety: [AS. hlot; akin to hletan to cast lots, OS. hlōt lot, D. lot, G. loos, OHG. lōz, Icel. hlutr, Sw. lott, Dan. lod, Goth. hlauts. Cf. Allot, Lotto, Lottery.]

defs: That which happens without human design or forethought; chance; accident; hazard; fortune; fate.
quotes: But save my life, which lot before your foot doth lay.

defs: Anything (as a die, pebble, ball, or slip of paper) used in determining a question by chance, or without man's choice or will; as, to cast or draw lots.
quotes: The lot is cast into the lap, but the whole disposing thereof is of the Lord.
If we draw lots, he speeds.

defs: The part, or fate, which falls to one, as it were, by chance, or without his planning.
quotes: O visions ill foreseen! Each day's lot's Enough to bear.
He was but born to try The lot of man -- to suffer and to die.

defs: A separate portion; a number of things taken collectively; all objects sold in a single purchase transaction; as, a lot of stationery; -- colloquially, sometimes of people; as, a sorry lot; a bad lot.
quotes: I, this winter, met with a very large lot of English heads, chiefly of the reign of James I.

defs: A distinct portion or plot of land, usually smaller than a field; as, a building lot in a city.
quotes: The defendants leased a house and lot in the city of New York.

defs: A large quantity or number; a great deal; as, to spend a lot of money; to waste a lot of time on line; lots of people think so.
quotes: He wrote to her . . . he might be detained in London by a lot of business.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Lotted (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Lotting (?).]

defs: To allot; to sort; to portion.
